2606.28692.title: An AI agent for treatment reasoning over a biomedical tool universe
2606.28692.paper_summary: Treatment reasoning underpins every therapeutic decision, integrating disease context, comorbidities, medications, contraindications, and evolving biomedical knowledge to select an appropriate therapy. It is inherently iterative: candidates are weighed against many constraints, revised as evidence emerges, and grounded in verifiable sources. Here we introduce ATHENA-R1, an AI agent for treatment reasoning across all FDA approved drugs since 1939, trained by reinforcement learning over a universe of 212 biomedical tools. At each step it identifies missing information, selects and runs relevant tools, and incorporates the evidence. To train it without human-annotated traces, we build a two-level self-learning framework: multi-agent systems construct the tools, tasks, and reasoning trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, then reinforcement learning with scientific feedback rewards reasoning quality (evidence gathering, grounded tool use, logical non-redundancy). Across five benchmarks of 3,168 drug reasoning tasks and 456 patient treatment cases, ATHENA-R1 outperforms language models and tool-use systems, reaching 94.7% accuracy on open-ended drug reasoning and 82.9% on treatment reasoning, 17.8 and 10.7 points above GPT-5. In blinded evaluations by experts from 28 rare disease organizations, it is preferred over reference models on all criteria, and physicians rated it favorably on complex hospitalized cardiovascular and infectious-disease cases. Adverse-event hypotheses it generated, tested in electronic health records from 5.4 million patients, reached adjusted odds ratios of 1.48-1.84, with no elevation among negative controls. Because it requires knowing what evidence to seek before concluding, treatment reasoning has long been hard for AI; we show it can be reframed as a learnable process of iterative evidence gathering that reinforcement learning can train AI to perform.
2606.28692.paper_summary_zh: 治療推理是每個治療決策的基礎，整合疾病背景、共病、藥物、禁忌症及不斷演變的生物醫學知識，以選擇適當的治療。這本質上是迭代的：候選者在許多限制下進行權衡，隨著證據的出現進行修訂，並根據可驗證的來源進行基礎建設。在此，我們介紹ATHENA-R1，一個針對自1939年以來所有FDA批准藥物的治療推理AI代理，通過強化學習在212個生物醫學工具的宇宙中進行訓練。在每一步中，它識別缺失的信息，選擇並運行相關工具，並整合證據。為了在沒有人工標註痕跡的情況下訓練它，我們建立了一個兩層自學框架：多代理系統構建工具、任務和推理軌跡以進行監督微調，然後通過科學反饋的強化學習獎勵推理質量（證據收集、基於工具的使用、邏輯非冗餘性）。在3168個藥物推理任務和456個患者治療案例的五個基準測試中，ATHENA-R1的表現超過了語言模型和工具使用系統，在開放式藥物推理中達到94.7%的準確率，在治療推理中達到82.9%，比GPT-5高出17.8和10.7個點。在來自28個罕見疾病組織的專家盲評中，它在所有標準上均優於參考模型，醫生在複雜的住院心血管和傳染病案例中對其評價良好。它生成的不良事件假設，在540萬患者的電子健康記錄中進行測試，達到1.48-1.84的調整比率，負控制組中無升高。由於治療推理需要在得出結論之前知道要尋求什麼證據，這使得AI長期以來難以實現；我們展示了它可以被重新框架為一個可學習的迭代證據收集過程，強化學習可以訓練AI來執行。
2606.28692.author: Shanghua Gao et.al.
2606.28692.authors: Shanghua Gao, Ayush Noori, Richard Zhu, Curtis Ginder, Zhenglun Kong, Xiaorui Su, Justin Kauffman, Benjamin S. Glicksberg, Joshua Lampert, Ankit Sakhuja, Ashwin Sawant, ATHENA-R1 Evaluation Consortium, David A. Clifton, Noa Dagan, Ran Balicer, Marinka Zitnik
